name: Rust Bebop decode performance vs C

description: Baseline: total decode across all scenarios

asi.hypothesis: Baseline measurement of all decode scenario times

description: Add #[inline(always)] to all reader primitives + read_n<N> const-generic for single bounds check

asi.hypothesis: Cross-crate inlining was missing. Adding #[inline(always)] on all reader methods + read_n<const N> to eliminate redundant bounds checks (ensure+slice+try_into replaced by single check+ptr read)

description: Add #[inline(always)] to FixedScalar/BebopFlagBits trait impl methods

asi.hypothesis: FixedScalar/BebopFlagBits impl methods missing #[inline] could hurt read_fixed_array and fallback paths
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise, no measurable improvement
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on higher-level optimizations: reduce allocations, optimize message/union tag loops

description: Optimize read_byte/read_bool (avoid checked_add, use get_unchecked) + read_str/read_string (combine bounds checks, use get_unchecked)

asi.hypothesis: read_byte (used for every message tag) has unnecessary checked_add overhead. read_str has redundant ensure+slice bounds checks. Using get_unchecked after manual check reduces per-call overhead.

description: Add #[inline] to from_bytes + optimize read_raw_bytes with get_unchecked

asi.hypothesis: from_bytes inline + read_raw_bytes get_unchecked might reduce overhead for byte-heavy types
asi.rollback_reason: No measurable improvement, within noise
asi.next_action_hint: Try code generator optimizations or look at the EmbeddingBatch decode pattern for array-of-struct improvements

description: Add #[inline] to all generated decode methods (struct, message, union, enum) via code generator

asi.hypothesis: Generated decode methods without #[inline] cause function call overhead when called from arrays and nested types

description: Mark error path eof_error as #[cold] #[inline(never)] to improve branch prediction

asi.hypothesis: #[cold] error path should improve branch prediction by keeping fast path hot
asi.rollback_reason: Regression across board — Embeddings 2x worse, TreeWide +19%. #[inline(never)] may cause unfavorable code layout changes
asi.next_action_hint: Try read_str optimization for large strings or batch bounds checking in message decode

description: Single 12-byte read for timestamp/duration instead of two separate reads

asi.hypothesis: Combined 12-byte read for timestamp/duration saves 1 bounds check per call
asi.rollback_reason: No measurable improvement — timestamps aren't hot in current benchmarks
asi.next_action_hint: Try optimizing generated message decode pattern or look at HashMap alternative

description: Validated message boundary + combined next_message_tag with bounds-skip for tag reads

asi.hypothesis: Validating message body boundary upfront allows skipping per-tag bounds checks in the tag loop
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — on aarch64 M-series, branch prediction makes the read_byte bounds check effectively free. The extra validation in read_validated_message_end adds overhead.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus on reducing allocations or try SIMD UTF-8 validation. Or explore generated code for struct decode with batch reads.

description: Replace Vec::new()+try_reserve with Vec::with_capacity in read_array; HashMap::new()+try_reserve with HashMap::with_capacity in read_map

asi.hypothesis: try_reserve has overhead from error-handling path and Result wrapping. with_capacity is simpler (panics on OOM instead of returning error)

description: Use simdutf8 for SIMD-accelerated UTF-8 validation in read_str and read_string

asi.hypothesis: simdutf8 uses NEON SIMD instructions for UTF-8 validation on aarch64, dramatically faster than core::str::from_utf8 for large buffers

description: Use #[inline(always)] on generated struct decode methods (structs are small, benefit from forced inlining)

asi.hypothesis: #[inline(always)] on struct decode forces inlining even in array contexts where compiler might not inline

description: Use #[inline(always)] on generated enum decode methods (enums are trivial)

asi.hypothesis: Enum decode methods are trivial (1 byte read + match), always inlining avoids call overhead in struct/message decoders

description: Add #[inline] to all writer methods + with_capacity in scalar_array fallbacks

asi.hypothesis: Writer inline annotations might improve icache locality for decode by making encode code smaller. Scalar array fallback with_capacity avoids try_reserve overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement, likely code bloat from too much inlining hurt instruction cache performance
asi.next_action_hint: Try focusing on a single hot path optimization, maybe read_array closure elimination or enum TryFrom optimization

description: Split UTF-8 validation: std for <64 bytes, simdutf8 for >=64 bytes

asi.hypothesis: Short strings might be faster with std from_utf8 vs simdutf8 due to SIMD setup overhead
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — extra branch adds overhead, simdutf8 already handles short strings well
asi.next_action_hint: Try batch struct decode for fixed-size structs, or focus on reducing allocations in recursive types

description: Add read_decode_array (direct trait dispatch instead of closure) for struct array decode

asi.hypothesis: Direct trait dispatch in read_decode_array might avoid closure overhead
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — Rust's monomorphization already inlines the closure effectively, no actual closure indirection
asi.next_action_hint: Reaching diminishing returns on low-level optimizations. Remaining bottlenecks are structural: HashMap SipHash, recursive allocations, UTF-8 validation. Consider the batch struct decode with enum support or accept current 42.5% improvement.

description: Use simdutf8 with default features instead of aarch64_neon-only

asi.hypothesis: Default simdutf8 features might auto-detect NEON
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement, within noise. aarch64_neon feature is already optimal.
asi.next_action_hint: Exhausted easy optimizations. Consider batch struct decode with enum support, or accept current 42.5% improvement.

description: Add #[inline] to generated encode/encoded_size methods

asi.hypothesis: Encode/encoded_size inline annotations might improve icache locality by making encode code smaller and putting decode code in a better position
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — encode code layout doesn't affect decode performance
asi.next_action_hint: We've exhausted easy optimizations. Current best is 42.5% improvement. Remaining gains require HashMap type changes or batch struct decode with enum support.

description: Use simdutf8::basic::from_utf8 directly as simd_from_utf8 returning &str (avoid separate validate + unsafe unchecked)

asi.hypothesis: Using simdutf8::basic::from_utf8 directly avoids double-wrapping (validate bool + unsafe unchecked)
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — compiler already optimized the previous pattern equivalently
asi.next_action_hint: Try batch fixed-size struct decode with enum support, or try optimizing EmbeddingBatch by reducing per-element overhead in read_scalar_array

description: Batch struct decode: single ensure_available + unchecked reads for fixed-size structs (TextSpan: u32+u32+enum_u8=9 bytes)

asi.hypothesis: Batch bounds check (1 ensure instead of 3 per TextSpan) should save 2 checks × 137 spans = 274 bounds checks in ChunkedText
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise. TextSpan decode is only ~500ns of ChunkedText's 2000ns total. Saving 2 bounds checks per span saves ~1ns each = ~137ns, lost in noise. The compiler may already merge consecutive bounds checks for inlined reads.
asi.next_action_hint: Try targeting the larger contributors: EmbeddingBatch (549ns, 8 array elements with overhead), or see if InferenceTiming can use batch reads (duration+duration+f32 = 28 bytes)

description: Enable thin LTO in release profile for cross-crate optimization

asi.hypothesis: ThinLTO enables cross-crate optimization that #[inline] alone might miss
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — our #[inline(always)] annotations already handle cross-crate inlining. LTO adds 4x build time with no decode benefit.
asi.next_action_hint: The easy optimizations are exhausted. Remaining bottlenecks: HashMap SipHash (JsonLarge), recursive Vec allocs (TreeDeep), simdutf8 ceiling (DocLarge/ChunkedText). Need structural changes or API breaks to go further.

description: Add read_cow_str convenience method and use in generated code for string arrays/map keys

asi.hypothesis: read_cow_str() convenience method simplifies generated code and may help compiler
asi.rollback_reason: Regression — extra function layer hurt. Compiler already optimized the inline Ok(Cow::Borrowed(read_str()?)) pattern well.
asi.next_action_hint: We're solidly in diminishing returns. Consider focusing on LLMChunkLarge string reads or accepting current 42.5% improvement.

description: Force #[inline(always)] on read_str (was only #[inline])

asi.hypothesis: read_str with #[inline] might not be inlined at all call sites due to simdutf8 call making it "too large". Forcing #[inline(always)] should eliminate call overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise — the compiler was already inlining read_str at the important call sites with just #[inline].
asi.next_action_hint: Try removing checked_mul in read_scalar_array on 64-bit, or look at the generated message decode path for TreeWide/TreeDeep.

description: Remove checked_mul in read_scalar_array on 64-bit platforms (overflow impossible)

asi.hypothesis: checked_mul in scalar array path adds unnecessary overflow check on 64-bit
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise — compiler likely already optimizes checked_mul to unchecked when it can prove no overflow via constant propagation of elem_size
asi.next_action_hint: Try generating message decoders without Default::default() init — use direct field assignment instead of mutating default

description: Force #[inline(always)] on validate_utf8 wrapper

asi.hypothesis: Force-inlining validate_utf8 might eliminate simdutf8 call overhead
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — simdutf8 is already effectively inlined by the compiler
asi.next_action_hint: The micro-optimization well is dry. Consider larger structural changes: switch to hashbrown HashMap (requires fixture changes), or try rewriting the message decode loop to avoid Default::default().

description: Message decode: use local Option variables instead of Default::default() + struct mutation

asi.hypothesis: Local Option vars give optimizer more freedom than Default struct + field mutation — no aliasing through struct reference
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — LLVM already optimizes the Default+mutation pattern equivalently to local vars. The patterns produce identical machine code.
asi.next_action_hint: Optimization plateau confirmed after 13 consecutive discards. Remaining bottlenecks (HashMap SipHash, recursive allocs, UTF-8 floor) require API-breaking changes.

description: Cache buf.len() as a struct field to avoid fat-pointer len loads in hot loops

asi.hypothesis: Caching buf.len() avoids repeated fat-pointer field loads, making it easier for LLVM to keep the length in a register across inlined reader calls
asi.rollback_reason: No improvement — LLVM already hoists buf.len() into a register across inlined code. The fat pointer load was not the bottleneck.
asi.next_action_hint: 14 consecutive discards. The micro-optimization well is truly dry. Only API-breaking changes (hashbrown HashMap, SmallVec) can improve further.

description: Force #[inline(always)] on generated message and union decode methods (was #[inline])

asi.hypothesis: Force-inlining message/union decode could eliminate call overhead for small messages (Bool, Number, String) used in JsonLarge
asi.rollback_reason: Regression in TreeWide (+10%) — message decoders with tag loops are too large to force-inline. Code bloat hurts icache on TreeWide's 100 message decodes.
asi.next_action_hint: 15 consecutive discards. This is definitively the optimization plateau. No further safe micro-optimizations are possible.

description: hashbrown HashMap (always use instead of std) — checks failed on import ordering

asi.hypothesis: Always use hashbrown::HashMap (foldhash) instead of std::collections::HashMap (SipHash)
asi.rollback_reason: rustfmt import ordering issue, fixing and re-running
asi.next_action_hint: Fix import order and retry

description: Always use hashbrown::HashMap (foldhash) instead of std HashMap (SipHash) — breaking API change

asi.hypothesis: hashbrown::HashMap uses foldhash which is much faster than SipHash for small keys. Map-heavy scenarios (JsonLarge, JsonSmall, DocumentSmall) should improve significantly.

description: ABLATION: Remove get_unchecked from read_byte/read_bool (use safe indexing)

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if get_unchecked in read_byte/read_bool is needed, or if LLVM eliminates bounds check after pos < buf.len()
asi.rollback_reason: +173ns (+1.5%) — borderline, within noise floor. Suggests get_unchecked may not be needed here.
asi.finding: Safe indexing after bounds check is likely equivalent — LLVM can prove no panic
asi.next_action_hint: Try ablation of get_unchecked in read_str/read_string too, then combined ablation

description: ABLATION: Remove ALL get_unchecked from read_byte/read_bool/read_str/read_string — safe indexing is equally fast

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test whether get_unchecked in read_byte/read_bool/read_str/read_string provides any benefit over safe indexing
asi.finding: LLVM eliminates redundant bounds checks after manual if/end checks. All get_unchecked in these functions was unnecessary unsafe code.
asi.simplification: Removed 4 unsafe blocks from hot paths with zero performance cost
asi.note: from_utf8_unchecked still kept since validate_utf8() is a separate function LLVM cannot prove guarantees from

description: ABLATION: Eliminate from_utf8_unchecked — use simdutf8's direct &str return. Removes 2 more unsafe blocks.

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: replace validate_utf8() bool + from_utf8_unchecked with simdutf8::basic::from_utf8() returning &str directly
asi.finding: Same performance within noise. simdutf8 already returns validated &str, so the unchecked conversion was redundant unsafe.
asi.simplification: Eliminated 2 more unsafe blocks (read_str, read_string). Only remaining unsafe in reader: read_n (ptr read), read_scalar_array (bulk memcpy).
asi.note: +1.6% within noise floor, code safety improvement justifies keeping

description: ABLATION: Replace unsafe ptr::read_unaligned in read_n with safe ensure+slice+try_into — eliminates another unsafe block

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if unsafe ptr::read_unaligned in read_n is needed, or if safe slice+try_into generates identical code
asi.finding: LLVM optimizes buf[pos..pos+N].try_into().unwrap() identically to ptr::read_unaligned when N is a const generic. No performance difference.
asi.simplification: Eliminated the core read_n unsafe block. Only remaining unsafe in reader: read_scalar_array (bulk memcpy for zero-copy arrays).

description: ABLATION: Downgrade all #[inline(always)] to #[inline] in reader.rs — compiler makes better inlining decisions

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if #[inline(always)] is needed on reader primitives, or if #[inline] gives LLVM enough freedom
asi.finding: #[inline] is actually slightly BETTER than #[inline(always)] (-1.6%). The forced inlining was causing minor code bloat that hurt icache. With #[inline], LLVM can choose not to inline in cold paths.
asi.simplification: Replaced 22 #[inline(always)] with #[inline] in reader.rs. Simpler, more idiomatic, and marginally faster.
asi.note: The original #[inline(always)] was needed when reader was in a separate crate without any inline hint. But #[inline] is sufficient for cross-crate inlining.

description: ABLATION: Downgrade #[inline(always)] to #[inline] on ALL generated struct+enum decode — regression

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if #[inline(always)] is needed on generated struct/enum decode methods
asi.rollback_reason: +3.9% regression. EventSmall +50% (enum decode overhead), TreeDeep +10.8%. Generated struct/enum decode methods need forced inlining.
asi.finding: #[inline(always)] is REQUIRED on generated struct/enum decode. These are called from arrays and nested types where the compiler won't inline without the hint.
asi.next_action_hint: Try split ablation: keep #[inline(always)] on enums, test downgrade only on structs

description: ABLATION: Downgrade struct decode from #[inline(always)] to #[inline] — only enums need forced inlining

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if struct decode needs #[inline(always)], or if only enum decode needs it
asi.finding: Struct decode works fine with #[inline]. The previous regression (run 36) was entirely from enum decode. Enums are tiny (1 byte + match) and must be force-inlined. Structs are larger and the compiler makes good decisions with just #[inline].
asi.simplification: Generator now uses #[inline] for struct decode, #[inline(always)] only for enum decode

description: ABLATION: Remove with_capacity from Vec and HashMap — massive regression confirms it's essential

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if Vec::with_capacity / HashMap::with_capacity matter vs Vec::new()
asi.rollback_reason: +16% total regression. LLMChunkLarge +68%, TreeWide +30%, TensorShardLarge +77%. Multiple reallocation+copy cycles are expensive.
asi.finding: with_capacity is ESSENTIAL. Cannot be simplified away. The count-prefixed wire format gives us exact sizes upfront — we must use them.

description: ABLATION: Replace simdutf8 with core::str::from_utf8 — massive regression confirms SIMD UTF-8 is essential

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if simdutf8 SIMD acceleration is needed vs core::str::from_utf8
asi.rollback_reason: +46% total regression. ChunkedText +116%, DocumentLarge +95%. SIMD UTF-8 validation is the single most important optimization.
asi.finding: simdutf8 is ESSENTIAL and CANNOT be simplified away. It provides 2x+ speedup for large string validation via NEON SIMD.

description: ABLATION: Remove #[inline] from message/union decode — regression confirms it's needed

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: test if #[inline] on message/union decode is needed or if compiler inlines cross-crate without the hint
asi.rollback_reason: +5.4% regression. JsonLarge +12%, LLMChunkLarge +11.5%, TensorShardSmall +124%. Without #[inline], cross-crate message decode is not inlined.
asi.finding: #[inline] on message/union decode is REQUIRED for cross-crate inlining. Tag loops make these look 'too large' to inline by default.

description: Try buf.get() in read_n instead of ensure+safe index — slight regression

asi.hypothesis: buf.get() might simplify read_n by combining bounds check and slice in one step
asi.rollback_reason: +3.1% regression. The ensure+index pattern generates slightly better code — LLVM can better optimize the separate check+index pattern.
asi.next_action_hint: Current code is already clean and fast. Focus on remaining forward optimizations.

description: Simplify ensure(): use wrapping_sub comparison instead of checked_add + pattern match

asi.hypothesis: Simpler ensure() without checked_add may generate tighter bounds-check code on the hot path
asi.finding: Two runs: 11114, 11231 (avg 11172). Previous best 11225. Marginal improvement within noise, but code is simpler — wrapping_sub comparison is one instruction vs checked_add+branch.
asi.note: wrapping_sub is safe because pos <= buf.len() is maintained by all advance methods

description: Replace read_byte/read_bool with read_n::<1>() — regression, hand-written single-byte path is faster

asi.hypothesis: Unify read_byte/read_bool through read_n::<1>() to simplify code
asi.rollback_reason: +5.8% total, TreeWide +62%. read_n::<1>() goes through ensure+slice+try_into which is heavier than direct if/index for single bytes. LLVM generates a simpler branch for the hand-written path.
asi.finding: Single-byte reads need dedicated paths. read_n<N> is optimal for N>=2 but adds overhead for N=1 due to slice creation.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the separate read_byte/read_bool implementations

description: read_array: use ptr::write + set_len instead of push — eliminates per-element capacity check

asi.hypothesis: Vec::push has a capacity check per element even after with_capacity. Using ptr::write + set_len eliminates this.
asi.finding: Consistent improvement across 3 runs (avg 11,183 vs 11,231 baseline). TreeWide -13% (100 array decodes, 100 eliminated capacity checks per decode). Proper error cleanup via set_len(i) on Err.
asi.note: Adds 1 unsafe block but with proper cleanup on error path

description: Inline length read in read_str — checks failed on formatting

asi.hypothesis: Inline the u32 length read in read_str to avoid read_u32 → read_n → ensure chain
asi.rollback_reason: rustfmt formatting issue, fixing and re-running
asi.next_action_hint: Fix formatting and retry

description: Inline u32 length read in read_str — no improvement, LLVM already inlines the chain

asi.hypothesis: Inlining the u32 read directly in read_str avoids function call overhead from read_u32 → read_n → ensure chain
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise (+0.3%). LLVM already inlines the entire chain with #[inline]. Manual inlining adds code complexity for zero benefit.
asi.finding: read_u32 → read_n → ensure is already fully inlined by LLVM in read_str context

description: Simplify read_map sanity check (remove /2 division) — no improvement, weakens safety

asi.hypothesis: Division in read_map sanity check might add overhead
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise (-0.5%). The /2 division is a one-time cost per map decode. The tighter check is more defensive against corrupt data.
asi.finding: read_map /2 check has negligible performance impact. Keep the tighter safety check.

description: Cleanup: use String::from instead of to_owned(), remove ToOwned import

asi.hypothesis: Code cleanup: String::from vs to_owned() should be identical codegen
asi.finding: Performance neutral as expected. Removes unnecessary ToOwned import for cleaner code.

description: Set codegen-units = 1 in release profile — allows LLVM full optimization scope

asi.hypothesis: codegen-units = 1 gives LLVM a single codegen unit per crate, enabling cross-function optimizations that 16 units can't do
asi.finding: Consistent -2.9% improvement (two runs: 10816, 10827). TreeWide -13%, InferenceResponse -16%, TreeDeep -7%. This is a general build configuration any user can adopt.
asi.tradeoff: ~2x longer build times in release mode
asi.note: Different from ThinLTO (which was tried and failed) — this is intra-crate optimization, not cross-crate

description: Add ThinLTO on top of codegen-units=1 — cross-crate optimization stacks with intra-crate

asi.hypothesis: ThinLTO + codegen-units=1 enables both intra-crate and cross-crate LLVM optimization. Previously ThinLTO alone failed because default codegen-units already limited intra-crate optimization.
asi.finding: Consistent -1.3% improvement (two runs: 10749, 10615). TreeDeep -7.1%, LLMChunkLarge -8.9%. Cross-crate optimization now stacks with intra-crate.
asi.note: Previous ThinLTO experiment (run 21) failed because codegen-units was default 16. The combination is key.

description: Try fat LTO instead of thin — regression, especially TreeDeep +61%

asi.hypothesis: Fat LTO might optimize better than ThinLTO with full cross-crate visibility
asi.rollback_reason: +10.4% total regression. TreeDeep 1422→2293 (+61%). Fat LTO makes poor inlining decisions for recursive types, causing code bloat that hurts icache.
asi.finding: ThinLTO is optimal. Fat LTO over-inlines recursive decode paths. Stick with ThinLTO.

description: Add strip=symbols to release profile — no runtime performance impact

asi.hypothesis: Stripping symbols reduces binary size which might improve icache behavior
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise. Symbol tables are in a separate section, not loaded into code cache. Stripping doesn't affect runtime performance.
asi.finding: strip=symbols has no runtime impact. Only useful for deployment binary size.

description: Add panic=abort to release profile — ~2% improvement but changes program semantics too much

asi.hypothesis: panic=abort eliminates unwinding infrastructure, making ? and unwrap() cheaper
asi.rollback_reason: While effective (-1.9% avg), panic=abort changes program semantics — no catch_unwind, no cleanup on panic. Too opinionated for a workspace default. Should be a user recommendation instead.
asi.finding: panic=abort provides ~2% improvement. Recommended for performance-critical deployments but not appropriate as a library default.
asi.user_recommendation: For maximum decode performance, add panic='abort' to [profile.release] in your Cargo.toml

description: Pre-validate message body in read_message_length — adds overhead, doesn't eliminate downstream checks

asi.hypothesis: Pre-validating entire message body with ensure(len) could help LLVM eliminate per-field bounds checks with ThinLTO
asi.rollback_reason: JsonLarge +6.7%, TreeDeep +7.4%. Extra ensure per message adds overhead. LLVM still can't prove downstream reads are safe because pos is mutated between reads.
asi.finding: Same as experiment 9 — redundant ensure on hot message decode path hurts. LLVM cannot propagate the pre-validation across pos mutations even with ThinLTO.
asi.next_action_hint: Message decode overhead is irreducible with current pattern. Only structural changes (skip tag loop for known-layout messages) could help.

description: Shrink DecodeError (usize→u32 for eof fields) — regression, Result layout changes hurt LLVM codegen

asi.hypothesis: Smaller DecodeError reduces Result<T> size from ~32 to ~24 bytes, potentially fitting in registers instead of sret
asi.rollback_reason: +6.2% total regression. TreeWide +17%, JsonLarge +13%, LLMChunkLarge +19%. The borderline Result size (between register and sret) causes LLVM to make suboptimal choices. The clearly-too-large-for-registers 32-byte Result with sret was actually well-optimized.
asi.finding: DecodeError size does NOT matter for performance. LLVM handles the large sret Result efficiently. Shrinking it to a borderline size hurts.

description: #[cold] #[inline(never)] on eof_error — still regresses even with ThinLTO

asi.hypothesis: Revisit #[cold] on error path now that ThinLTO + codegen-units=1 gives LLVM better layout control
asi.rollback_reason: +3.5% total. JsonLarge +13.5%, LLMChunkLarge +13.5%. #[inline(never)] forces error construction out-of-line, hurting register allocation. Same finding as experiment 7.
asi.finding: CONFIRMED: #[cold] on error path is a dead end regardless of LTO settings. LLVM handles error path layout better when it has full control.

description: Try pos != len instead of pos < len in read_byte — within noise, identical codegen

asi.hypothesis: != comparison might generate different branch condition (b.ne vs b.lo on aarch64)
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise. LLVM generates identical branch code for both patterns when inlined. Keep < for readability.
asi.finding: Comparison operator doesn't matter — LLVM optimizes both to equivalent branch on aarch64.

description: Use insert_unique_unchecked in read_map — no improvement, probing rarely compares keys

asi.hypothesis: insert_unique_unchecked skips duplicate key check, saving a key comparison per insert
asi.rollback_reason: Within noise. hashbrown's probing finds empty slots directly (avg ~1.14 probes). Key comparisons only happen on hash collisions, which are rare with good capacity.
asi.finding: HashMap insert overhead is dominated by hashing + memory allocation, not key comparison. Skipping duplicate check saves negligible time.

description: Build Vec of entries then collect to HashMap — regression from extra allocation

asi.hypothesis: Building entries in a Vec then collecting to HashMap might let hashbrown batch-insert more efficiently
asi.rollback_reason: JsonLarge +15.8%, JsonSmall +18.6%. Extra Vec allocation + from_iter overhead worse than direct insert loop. The with_capacity+insert pattern is already optimal.
asi.finding: Direct insert loop is better than Vec→collect for HashMap construction

description: FxHash instead of foldhash — abandoned, too invasive (changes HashMap type everywhere), marginal expected gain

asi.hypothesis: FxHash multiply+XOR might be faster than foldhash for short string keys
asi.rollback_reason: Changing the default hasher ripples through all fixture code, integration tests, and generated types. The expected gain for short keys is marginal — foldhash is already optimized for this use case. Abandoned without benchmarking due to invasiveness.
asi.finding: Hasher swaps require massive API surface changes. foldhash is already near-optimal for string keys. Not worth pursuing.

description: Baseline measurement — no code changes, confirming plateau at ~10,500ns (47.8% improvement)

asi.hypothesis: Baseline stability check
asi.rollback_reason: No code changes to keep — this is a measurement-only run
asi.finding: Plateau confirmed across 6 sessions. All safe non-structural optimizations exhausted. Remaining ~10,500ns is: UTF-8 floor ~4,800ns + TreeDeep allocs ~1,500ns + JsonLarge HashMap ~1,500ns + LLMChunkLarge strings ~1,200ns + TreeWide msgs ~670ns + everything else ~830ns
asi.next_action_hint: Only SmallVec (API change) or arena allocator (major engineering) can improve further

description: ABLATION: Replace read_array ptr::write/set_len with safe push — regression confirms unsafe is needed

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: replace unsafe ptr::write + set_len in read_array with safe Vec::push to see if capacity check overhead matters
asi.rollback_reason: +3.8% total regression. TreeWide 672→914 (+36%), LLMChunkLarge +7.8%. Per-element capacity check in push is measurable overhead when decoding arrays of 100+ elements.
asi.finding: read_array unsafe is CONFIRMED ESSENTIAL — cannot be removed

description: ABLATION: Replace read_scalar_array unaligned bulk memcpy with safe per-element reads — catastrophic regression

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: replace unsafe copy_nonoverlapping + set_len in read_scalar_array unaligned path with safe per-element T::read_from loop
asi.rollback_reason: Catastrophic +190% total regression. EmbeddingBatch 495→21,384 (+4200%), OrderLarge 44→211 (+380%). Per-element reads with bounds checks destroy performance for large scalar arrays.
asi.finding: read_scalar_array unaligned bulk memcpy is CONFIRMED ESSENTIAL — cannot be removed

description: ABLATION: Remove unsafe from write_scalar_array — safe per-element writes, zero decode impact

asi.hypothesis: Ablation: replace unsafe from_raw_parts in write_scalar_array with safe per-element writes
asi.finding: Writer unsafe has zero impact on decode performance (encode-only path). Safe to remove for code quality. May affect encode performance but that's not measured here.
asi.encode_caveat: Encode of large scalar arrays (EmbeddingBatch etc) may be slower — not measured by decode benchmark
